title: 1 A Preliminary Computational Study of the Language of the Hadith Reports in Kanz al-Ummāl
text:
دراسة حاسوبية أولية للألفاظ النبوية في كتاب كنز العمال




An evolving computational linguistics framework for constructing, curating, and analyzing a large Classical Arabic hadith corpus.


Version 1.0 · Proof of Concept

title: 1 A Preliminary Computational Study of the Language of the Hadith Reports in Kanz al-Ummāl

Current status. The corpus is sufficiently developed to demonstrate large-scale quantitative analysis, but it remains a preliminary computational corpus. The highest priority is continued curation of the hadith number → matn → source relationship.

title: 1 A Preliminary Computational Study of the Language of the Hadith Reports in Kanz al-Ummāl
text:
1.2 Explore the Project


1.2.1 Corpus Foundations
Understand the source text, the Shamela digital sources, and how the material is represented computationally.
Source Text & Digital Sources →
Computational Corpus →


1.2.2 Matn Statistics
Explore the first corpus-wide quantitative profile of the hadith text: lexical counts, particles, report lengths, lexical diversity, and descriptive distributions.
Open Matn Statistics →


1.2.3 Repeated Phrases
Examine repeated multi-word sequences across the corpus, including 2-grams through 5-grams, as a foundation for empirical study of formulaic language.
Open Repeated Phrases →


1.2.4 Sources & Narrators
Review recognized source abbreviations, source-frequency patterns, preliminary narrator extraction, and the cases that remain priorities for human review.
Open Sources & Narrators →


1.2.5 Future Computational Study
See the kinds of linguistic, morphological, statistical, and transmission-related questions that a more fully curated corpus could support.
Explore Future Directions →


1.2.6 Collaboration
Contributions are welcome from anyone interested in hadith studies, Classical Arabic, Arabic computational linguistics, corpus construction, or digital text curation.
The highest current priority is improving the underlying corpus by validating the relationship between hadith numbers, matn, and source citations.

title: 1 A Preliminary Computational Study of the Language of the Hadith Reports in Kanz al-Ummāl
text:
1.3 Project Scope
The project begins with the approximately 46,600 numbered hadith reports preserved in Kanz al-Ummāl. The current computational framework stores the corpus and derived analyses in DuckDB and supports reproducible corpus-level investigation.
Version 1.0 is intentionally a proof of concept, not a definitive digital edition. Its purpose is to demonstrate what becomes possible once a large Classical Arabic hadith collection is represented as structured computational data, while making the remaining curation needs explicit.

1.3.1 From Corpus Construction to Computational Inquiry
A carefully curated hadith number → matn → source table would strengthen every downstream analysis simultaneously—from lexical and formulaic studies to Arabic morphology, source profiling, narrator analysis, and eventually linkage to original source books containing fuller isnād information.

title: Source Abbreviations and Narrators
text:
Script 05 extends the Version 1.0 corpus from matn analysis to source-field analysis. It identifies source abbreviations, tallies their frequency across the corpus, creates a hadith-level source-abbreviation matrix, extracts preliminary narrator names from the source field, and produces audit tables for unresolved or ambiguous source tokens.
The script is designed as a conservative analytical layer. It does not attempt full isnād reconstruction. Instead, it separates what can be recognized reliably from what requires human review. This makes the current corpus useful for preliminary source and narrator analysis while preserving unresolved cases for later scholarly curation.



The script generates nine analytical products. Each output is exported as a CSV file and mirrored into DuckDB using the same kanz_05_* table name.







Output
Description




kanz_05_source_abbreviation_lookup
Lookup table of abbreviation variants, canonical abbreviations, source names, status, category, and notes.


kanz_05_source_abbreviation_counts
Corpus-wide frequency counts for recognized source abbreviations.


kanz_05_source_narrator_counts
Frequency counts of narrator names extracted from source-field patterns.


kanz_05_source_narrator_report_audit
Hadith-level audit table showing narrator spans, stop reasons, and associated source text.


kanz_05_source_token_category_counts
Counts of token categories observed while parsing the source field.


kanz_05_source_unknown_review_tokens
Tokens requiring review because they were not recognized as source abbreviations or standard formulae.


kanz_05_source_abbreviation_report_audit
Hadith-level audit table classifying source-field recognition status.


kanz_05_hadith_source_abbreviation_matrix
Hadith-by-source matrix showing source abbreviation counts per report.


kanz_05_source_analysis_run_summary
Overall processing summary for Script 05.









NoteDesign note



Script 05 combines two complementary tasks: source abbreviation recognition and preliminary narrator extraction. The abbreviation analysis is lookup-driven and relatively stable. The narrator extraction is intentionally heuristic and should be treated as a first-pass index for review rather than a completed isnād analysis.

Version 1.0 includes 46,623 hadith rows. Source text is present in 38,998 rows (83.6%), while 7,625 rows (16.4%) currently lack a source field. The lookup table contains 33 canonical source abbreviations derived from 36 lookup rows.
Recognized source abbreviations occur in 31,598 reports, representing 67.8% of all rows and 81.0% of rows with source text. Across the corpus, the script identifies 48,991 source-abbreviation instances.
Preliminary narrator extraction identifies 23,875 narrator instances, corresponding to 4,283 distinct normalized narrator names. Narrator information is extracted from 22,998 reports, or 59.0% of source-present rows.

1.3%














The audit table distinguishes several levels of confidence. Reports with recognized abbreviations can be used immediately for preliminary source-frequency analysis. Reports with recognized abbreviations plus review tokens are partially interpretable but should remain flagged. Reports with source text but no detected abbreviation require closer inspection, since they may contain narrator-only material, non-standard formatting, or source forms not yet captured in the lookup table.

Narrator extraction is intentionally preliminary. The current rule captures phrases following عن until the end of the source field or until a recognized source abbreviation is encountered. This is useful for building an initial narrator index, but it does not yet resolve name variants, patronymics, ambiguous shortened names, or full isnād chains. The resulting table should therefore be understood as a reviewable working index rather than a finalized biographical authority file.

The review-token output is important for iterative corpus improvement. Rather than hiding unrecognized material, the script explicitly surfaces unresolved tokens so that the source lookup table can be refined over time. This allows manual review to be focused on the highest-yield cases first.



Script 05 demonstrates that the source field is already sufficiently structured to support preliminary quantitative analysis. A large majority of source-present reports contain recognized abbreviations, and the source lookup framework makes it possible to convert compact editorial symbols into analyzable source categories.
The narrator extraction results are promising but should be interpreted cautiously. They show that narrator information can be recovered at scale from recurring source-field patterns, but the output remains dependent on heuristic name-span extraction. This is useful for exploratory analysis and prioritizing manual review, but not yet sufficient for full isnād reconstruction.
The most important contribution of this stage is not simply the frequency counts themselves, but the creation of a reviewable source-analysis layer. Recognized abbreviations, narrator spans, unknown tokens, and audit statuses are all preserved in separate tables. This makes the corpus both analytically useful and incrementally correctable.






ImportantKey point



Script 05 supports preliminary source and narrator analysis, but it also identifies where human curation would have the greatest effect: unresolved source tokens, source-present rows without detected abbreviations, and narrator-name normalization.





The Script 05 outputs provide the foundation for several future lines of analysis:

source-frequency profiling across the corpus;
identification of dominant source collections and compilers;
source co-occurrence analysis using the hadith-by-source matrix;
preliminary narrator frequency analysis;
narrator-name normalization and authority-table construction;
focused manual review of unresolved source tokens;
comparison of source patterns across thematic or structural subsets of the corpus;
future transmission-network analysis after additional curation;
eventual isnād reconstruction once source segmentation and narrator normalization are more mature.

At the current Version 1.0 stage, source abbreviation analysis is more reliable than narrator reconstruction. The best near-term use of these results is therefore exploratory source profiling and targeted corpus curation, rather than definitive claims about transmission networks.

title: Source Abbreviations and Narrators
Matn Statistics
text:
This script performs the first large-scale quantitative analysis of the hadith text (matn) after construction of the preliminary corpus. Rather than examining individual reports, it summarizes the linguistic characteristics of the corpus as a whole and produces reusable statistical tables for subsequent analyses. All outputs are written both as CSV files and as mirrored DuckDB tables, allowing later scripts to query the results directly without recomputation.
The script also reflects an important design principle of the pipeline: different linguistic phenomena are treated separately rather than merged into a single undifferentiated word count. Lexical vocabulary, independent particles (ḥurūf), and selected devotional formulae are counted independently. This allows later studies to focus on vocabulary, grammar, or formulaic language without repeating the preprocessing step.



The script generates the following analytical products. Each output is exported as a CSV file and mirrored into DuckDB, making the results immediately available for downstream statistical analyses.



Script 04 analytical outputs






Output
Description




kanz_04_matn_hadith_level_stats
Per-hadith descriptive statistics, including word counts, character counts, lexical diversity, sentence estimates, token lists, and normalized matn.


kanz_04_matn_lexical_word_counts
Frequency table of lexical words across the corpus.


kanz_04_matn_unconnected_huruf_counts
Counts of independent Arabic particles (ḥurūf) using a reference lookup table.


kanz_04_matn_pious_phrase_counts
Counts of selected devotional formulae preserved as single textual units.


kanz_04_matn_length_statistics
Summary statistics describing corpus-wide length and token distributions.


kanz_04_matn_length_quantiles
Quantile distribution of report lengths and related measures.


kanz_04_source_code_length_summary
Summary of report lengths grouped by source classification.


kanz_04_run_summary
Overall processing summary for the corpus.

NoteKey finding



The preliminary Version 1.0 corpus already contains more than one million lexical words distributed across more than forty-six thousand hadith records. This provides a substantial proof-of-concept foundation for quantitative corpus analysis, even though the underlying hadith number–matn–source table still requires further curation.

title: Matn Statistics
text:
3 Preliminary Results
The current Version 1.0 corpus contains 46,623 hadith records, of which 43,846 (94.0%) currently contain matn text suitable for quantitative analysis. Across these reports, the corpus contains approximately 1,266,206 textual units, including approximately 1,058,736 lexical words and 207,454 independent Arabic particles. The lexical vocabulary presently consists of 83,280 distinct word forms.
These figures indicate that the preliminary corpus is already large enough to support corpus-level descriptive analysis. Basic lexical statistics, length distributions, and particle frequencies can now be computed reproducibly across tens of thousands of reports.

Report length is highly variable, reflecting the heterogeneous nature of Kanz al-Ummāl. The median report contains 17 textual units (14 lexical words), while the mean report contains approximately 27.2 textual units (22.7 lexical words). This difference between the median and mean shows that the distribution is right-skewed: most reports are relatively short, while a smaller number of very long reports raise the arithmetic average.
The interquartile range extends from approximately 10 to 31 textual units. In practical terms, the middle half of the corpus consists of reports in this range. The longest report contains 2,925 textual units, and the longest lexical-word count is 2,458. For this reason, future analyses should report medians and quantiles rather than relying only on arithmetic means.

The quantile table provides a compact way to understand what is typical in the corpus. Half of all reports contain 17 or fewer textual units, while the upper tail contains relatively few but substantially longer narrations.

title: Matn Statistics
text:
5 Distribution of Matn Lengths









The histogram illustrates the strongly right-skewed nature of report lengths. Most narrations are comparatively short, while progressively fewer reports occupy the long tail of the distribution. The plot is truncated at 200 textual units to make the main distribution readable; the full corpus contains a small number of much longer reports.
This pattern is expected in large hadith compilations, where many reports consist of brief statements, rulings, or narrative fragments, while a smaller number preserve extended narrations. Analytically, this means that later statistical models and stylometric comparisons should be designed with skewed text lengths in mind.

Independent particles account for approximately 16.4% of all textual units. This is important for Arabic analysis because particles carry significant grammatical and discourse functions. Counting them separately makes it possible to study Arabic function-word behavior without confusing it with lexical vocabulary.
The current Version 1.0 script does not split connected prefixes such as و-, ف-, ب-, ل-, and ك-. The reported particle counts should therefore be understood as counts of independent particles only. This is a conservative choice and avoids overinterpreting attached forms before a dedicated Arabic morphological layer is added.

Selected devotional formulae are protected and counted as single textual units. This prevents blessings, peace formulae, divine honorifics, and companion formulae from being fragmented into ordinary word counts. The result is a cleaner separation between the lexical content of the matn and recurring formulaic expressions that serve religious, devotional, or editorial functions.

The source-code summary provides a preliminary way to compare report lengths across source-classification categories. At this stage, these comparisons should be read cautiously because source classification remains under refinement. However, the table is useful as a quality-control tool and can help identify categories that contain unusually long or short records.

title: Matn Statistics
text:
10 Interpretation
This stage demonstrates that the preliminary corpus is already suitable for corpus-level descriptive analysis. Basic lexical statistics, report-length distributions, and Arabic particle frequencies can now be computed reproducibly across more than forty-six thousand hadith records.
An important design principle of the pipeline is the explicit separation of lexical vocabulary, independent particles (ḥurūf), and devotional formulae. Rather than collapsing all tokens into a single frequency table, these linguistic categories are preserved independently. This allows future studies to investigate vocabulary, grammatical structure, and formulaic language without additional preprocessing.
At the same time, these statistics should be regarded as preliminary because they reflect the current state of the corpus. Future refinement of report segmentation or source extraction may produce modest changes in individual counts. However, the overall statistical profile of the corpus is unlikely to change substantially unless large-scale changes are made to the underlying hadith number–matn–source table.

title: Matn Statistics
text:
11 Building on These Results
Although this script performs only descriptive statistics, it establishes the foundation for a broad range of computational analyses. Reasonable extensions include:

Lexical profiling: identifying the most characteristic vocabulary of the corpus.
Formulaic language studies: examining recurring expressions and patterns of religious discourse.
Arabic function-word analysis: using particle frequencies to investigate stylistic and grammatical variation.
Vocabulary richness: comparing lexical diversity across subsets of the corpus.
Report-length modeling: characterizing the distribution of short and long traditions.
Corpus comparison: enabling future comparisons with other hadith collections or Classical Arabic texts.
Statistical and machine-learning applications: using normalized lexical frequency tables as input for clustering, topic discovery, semantic modeling, and information retrieval.







ImportantPractical conclusion



For Version 1.0, the main conclusion is that the computational framework is functional as a proof of concept. The strongest next step is not to redesign this analysis, but to improve the underlying hadith number–matn–source table through systematic corpus curation.

title: Matn Statistics
section: 12 Template Pattern for Later Script Sections
text:
12 Template Pattern for Later Script Sections
The same report structure can be reused for subsequent post-corpus scripts:

Purpose — what question the script answers and why it matters.
Outputs — CSV files and mirrored DuckDB tables created by the script.
Preliminary results — the most important numerical or qualitative findings.
Tables and figures — generated directly from current output files.
Interpretation — what the results mean and what cautions apply.
Building on these results — practical computational analyses made possible by the script.

title: Source Text and Digital Sources
text:
The computational corpus described in this report is derived from Kanz al-Ummāl fī Sunan al-Aqwāl wa al-Afʿāl.
Bibliographic information

Title: كنز العمال في سنن الأقوال والأفعال
Author: علاء الدين علي المتقي بن حسام الدين الهندي (d. 975 AH)
Edited by: الشيخ بكري حياني
Indexes and verification: الشيخ صفوة السقا
Publisher: مؤسسة الرسالة
Edition: Fifth edition (1405 AH / 1985 CE)
Volumes: 18 (16 text volumes plus indexes)

The edition contains an author’s introduction followed by the hadith corpus. The first hadith report begins on printed page 21 and the final report appears on printed page 12,325. Hadith reports are numbered sequentially from 1 through 46,624, although some numbers are intentionally absent from the printed edition and are discussed later in this report.
Conceptually, each report consists of three principal components:

Hadith number.
Matn (the text of the report).
Source citations identifying the original compilations from which the report was drawn.

Editorial section titles and subsection headings are interspersed throughout the printed work and are not themselves hadith reports.



Two complementary digital representations of Kanz al-Ummāl were used during development.


The first source consisted of a locally archived copy of the online Shamela edition (https://shamela.ws/book/2677). A custom Python scraper downloaded each printed page individually and stored both the original HTML and a plain-text representation. The scraper supports checkpointing, resumable execution, randomized request delays, and incremental downloading, allowing the complete corpus to be acquired reliably over multiple sessions.
The HTML archive contains all printed pages containing hadith reports and served as the primary development and validation corpus during the early stages of parser construction.



The second source was the Shamela4 Full Database distributed through Hugging Face:
https://huggingface.co/datasets/AuthenticIlm/Shamela4_Full_DB
For this project only the Kanz al-Ummāl portion of the database was imported:
https://huggingface.co/datasets/AuthenticIlm/Shamela4_Full_DB/tree/main/06__كتب-السنة/1654__كنز-العمال-في-سنن-الأقوال-والأفعال-ط-الرسالة
Unlike the HTML archive, the database stores the text as JSON Lines (JSONL), where each physical printed page is represented by a single JSON object. The principal file used for corpus construction is pages.jsonl, which contains one record for every printed page.
The most important fields include:



Field
Description




page_id
Unique page identifier


book_id
Shamela book identifier


part
Printed volume


page_num
Printed page number


sequence_num
Sequential page order


body
Main page text


footnotes
Editorial footnotes


hints
Auxiliary metadata


services_raw
Additional metadata



Inspection of the source data shows that the principal textual content is stored in the body field. Editorial headings are embedded within the page text using HTML span elements and therefore require special handling during corpus construction to prevent them from being interpreted as hadith text.




The HTML archive and the structured JSONL database represent the same printed edition but serve complementary purposes.
The HTML archive proved valuable during early parser development and manual validation because it closely reflects the appearance of the online edition. The structured JSONL database subsequently became the preferred source for corpus construction because it provides explicit page-level metadata and supports efficient import into DuckDB.
Throughout development, the HTML archive continued to serve as an independent reference when investigating ambiguous parsing cases, while the JSONL database became the canonical source for automated corpus construction.

title: Source Text and Digital Sources
text:
The computational corpus described in this report is derived from Kanz al-Ummāl fī Sunan al-Aqwāl wa al-Afʿāl.
Bibliographic information

Title: كنز العمال في سنن الأقوال والأفعال
Author: علاء الدين علي المتقي بن حسام الدين الهندي (d. 975 AH)
Edited by: الشيخ بكري حياني
Indexes and verification: الشيخ صفوة السقا
Publisher: مؤسسة الرسالة
Edition: Fifth edition (1405 AH / 1985 CE)
Volumes: 18 (16 text volumes plus indexes)

The edition contains an author’s introduction followed by the hadith corpus. The first hadith report begins on printed page 21 and the final report appears on printed page 12,325. Hadith reports are numbered sequentially from 1 through 46,624, although some numbers are intentionally absent from the printed edition and are discussed later in this report.
Conceptually, each report consists of three principal components:

Hadith number.
Matn (the text of the report).
Source citations identifying the original compilations from which the report was drawn.

Editorial section titles and subsection headings are interspersed throughout the printed work and are not themselves hadith reports.

title: Source Text and Digital Sources
text:
Two complementary digital representations of Kanz al-Ummāl were used during development.


The first source consisted of a locally archived copy of the online Shamela edition (https://shamela.ws/book/2677). A custom Python scraper downloaded each printed page individually and stored both the original HTML and a plain-text representation. The scraper supports checkpointing, resumable execution, randomized request delays, and incremental downloading, allowing the complete corpus to be acquired reliably over multiple sessions.
The HTML archive contains all printed pages containing hadith reports and served as the primary development and validation corpus during the early stages of parser construction.



The second source was the Shamela4 Full Database distributed through Hugging Face:
https://huggingface.co/datasets/AuthenticIlm/Shamela4_Full_DB
For this project only the Kanz al-Ummāl portion of the database was imported:
https://huggingface.co/datasets/AuthenticIlm/Shamela4_Full_DB/tree/main/06__كتب-السنة/1654__كنز-العمال-في-سنن-الأقوال-والأفعال-ط-الرسالة
Unlike the HTML archive, the database stores the text as JSON Lines (JSONL), where each physical printed page is represented by a single JSON object. The principal file used for corpus construction is pages.jsonl, which contains one record for every printed page.
The most important fields include:

page_id

Inspection of the source data shows that the principal textual content is stored in the body field. Editorial headings are embedded within the page text using HTML span elements and therefore require special handling during corpus construction to prevent them from being interpreted as hadith text.

title: Source Text and Digital Sources
text:
The HTML archive and the structured JSONL database represent the same printed edition but serve complementary purposes.
The HTML archive proved valuable during early parser development and manual validation because it closely reflects the appearance of the online edition. The structured JSONL database subsequently became the preferred source for corpus construction because it provides explicit page-level metadata and supports efficient import into DuckDB.
Throughout development, the HTML archive continued to serve as an independent reference when investigating ambiguous parsing cases, while the JSONL database became the canonical source for automated corpus construction.

title: Computational Corpus
text:
The canonical computational corpus is maintained in a DuckDB database. DuckDB stores the parsed hadith corpus together with audit tables, intermediate products, and downstream analytical results. This architecture provides a reproducible workflow from the original digital source files to the statistical analyses presented later in this report.
The principal analytical table is:
kanz_hadith_reports
Conceptually, each record contains three components:

Hadith number
Matn (text)
Source citations






Although this structure appears straightforward, several characteristics of the Shamela digitalization complicate automatic corpus construction.



Rather than relying on a single parsing pass, the project adopts an audit-driven approach. Independent quality-control procedures are used to identify ambiguous records before they enter the canonical corpus.
This philosophy favors conservative extraction over aggressive automation. When uncertainty exists, the record is retained for later review rather than forcing an automatic decision.



The printed edition does not contain a perfectly continuous sequence of hadith numbers. During development, a dedicated audit compared the extracted numbering with the original source text to distinguish genuine numbering irregularities from parser failures.
The primary purpose of this audit was validation rather than correction. It demonstrated that numbering anomalies are properties of the source edition itself and therefore should not automatically be interpreted as parser errors.
Future versions of the corpus are expected to rely primarily on a manually curated numbering table, making the detailed audit outputs largely historical.



During corpus construction, the hadith numbering sequence was audited against the source text. The purpose of this audit was not to produce a final corrected edition, but to determine whether apparent gaps and anomalies were parser artifacts or features already present in the source edition.
The audit reads the CSV outputs stored under:
numbering_audit_dir <- file.path(
  "kanz_outputs",
  "02a_missing_hadith_number_audit"
)



Summary of the hadith numbering audit.


Measure
Value




Expected hadith numbering range
1–46,624


Observed hadith starts in source
44,092


Missing numbers checked
2,582


Missing numbers present as literal hadith starts
0


Missing numbers absent as literal hadith starts
2,582


Consecutive missing-number ranges
159


Likely local source misnumberings
7


Source numbering gaps
152


Duplicate-number rows inspected
0





The numbering audit demonstrates that apparent gaps in the hadith sequence are not simply parser failures. Missing report numbers were checked directly against the source text, and the audit distinguishes numbers that are literally absent from the source from those requiring further review.
For the purposes of this Version 1.0 report, the detailed gap structure is less important than the general conclusion: the parser cannot assume a perfectly continuous sequence from 1 to 46,624. Numbering irregularities must be explicitly audited and, eventually, resolved through a curated reference table.






NoteInterpretation



The numbering audit is primarily a validation layer. It documents that some numbering irregularities originate in the source edition itself and should not be treated automatically as extraction failures.
In later versions of the project, this audit can be reduced or replaced once a curated hadith number table exists.





A more substantial challenge concerns identification of the boundary between the matn and the abbreviated source citations.
Although most reports follow the general pattern

hadith number → matn → source citations

the editorial conventions used to indicate this transition are not completely standardized. Source citations may appear parenthetically, within quotation marks, or as bare abbreviations embedded directly in the running text. No single parsing rule correctly identifies every transition.
Accordingly, the Version 1.0 parser adopts a conservative strategy. When the boundary cannot be determined confidently, potentially ambiguous material is preserved for subsequent scholarly review rather than being split automatically.



A second audit examined the abbreviated source citations that follow the matn. Unlike the hadith numbering audit, this stage focuses on the transition between the text of the report and the bibliographic information identifying its original sources.
The audit summarizes recognized source abbreviations, narrator extraction, unresolved source tokens, and report-level parsing decisions. Together these provide an overall assessment of the reliability of source identification in the current Version 1.0 corpus.



Summary of the source citation audit.


Measure
Value




Recognized source abbreviations
36


Distinct source abbreviations observed
33


Unknown source-review tokens
1182


Distinct narrators extracted
4283


Reports containing source abbreviations
46,623


Hadith/source matrix rows
46,623





The audit demonstrates that most source citations can be recognized using a controlled lookup table of standard abbreviations. Nevertheless, the printed edition does not employ a completely uniform editorial style, and some reports contain abbreviated forms or formatting patterns that cannot yet be classified automatically.
Rather than forcing uncertain classifications, the parser records these unresolved cases for subsequent scholarly review. This conservative strategy preserves the integrity of the corpus while simultaneously identifying the specific records that would benefit most from manual curation.






ImportantA Curated Source Table



The greatest remaining opportunity for improving the computational corpus is the creation of a manually curated hadith number → matn → source reference table.
Such a resource would establish definitive boundaries between the text of each hadith and its accompanying source citations. Once available, it would immediately improve lexical analyses, repeated phrase discovery, narrator extraction, source attribution, transmission-network reconstruction, stylometric studies, and virtually every downstream computational linguistic analysis.
The present audit framework is designed to facilitate that process by identifying precisely those records requiring scholarly review rather than attempting increasingly complex parsing heuristics.











ImportantCollaborative Corpus Curation



The principal remaining challenge is no longer demonstrating that large-scale computational analysis of Kanz al-Ummāl is feasible. Version 1.0 establishes that proof of concept.
The next stage of the project is the creation of a carefully curated hadith number → matn → source reference table.
Such a curated table would immediately improve every downstream analysis, including lexical statistics, repeated phrase discovery, source abbreviation analysis, narrator extraction, stylometric studies, transmission-network analysis, and future computational linguistics research.
For this reason, scholarly corpus curation is expected to provide substantially greater long-term benefit than continued refinement of individual parsing heuristics.





Despite the remaining areas requiring curation, the current Version 1.0 corpus already supports meaningful quantitative investigation. The following chapters present three examples of corpus-wide analyses generated directly from the current DuckDB corpus: descriptive statistics of the matn, empirical discovery of repeated phrases, and analysis of source abbreviations and narrator information.

text:
A second audit examined the abbreviated source citations that follow the matn. Unlike the hadith numbering audit, this stage focuses on the transition between the text of the report and the bibliographic information identifying its original sources.
The audit summarizes recognized source abbreviations, narrator extraction, unresolved source tokens, and report-level parsing decisions. Together these provide an overall assessment of the reliability of source identification in the current Version 1.0 corpus.



Summary of the source citation audit.


Measure
Value




Recognized source abbreviations
36


Distinct source abbreviations observed
33


Unknown source-review tokens
1182


Distinct narrators extracted
4283


Reports containing source abbreviations
46,623


Hadith/source matrix rows
46,623





The audit demonstrates that most source citations can be recognized using a controlled lookup table of standard abbreviations. Nevertheless, the printed edition does not employ a completely uniform editorial style, and some reports contain abbreviated forms or formatting patterns that cannot yet be classified automatically.
Rather than forcing uncertain classifications, the parser records these unresolved cases for subsequent scholarly review. This conservative strategy preserves the integrity of the corpus while simultaneously identifying the specific records that would benefit most from manual curation.






ImportantA Curated Source Table



The greatest remaining opportunity for improving the computational corpus is the creation of a manually curated hadith number → matn → source reference table.
Such a resource would establish definitive boundaries between the text of each hadith and its accompanying source citations. Once available, it would immediately improve lexical analyses, repeated phrase discovery, narrator extraction, source attribution, transmission-network reconstruction, stylometric studies, and virtually every downstream computational linguistic analysis.
The present audit framework is designed to facilitate that process by identifying precisely those records requiring scholarly review rather than attempting increasingly complex parsing heuristics.

title: Computational Corpus
text:
ImportantCollaborative Corpus Curation



The principal remaining challenge is no longer demonstrating that large-scale computational analysis of Kanz al-Ummāl is feasible. Version 1.0 establishes that proof of concept.
The next stage of the project is the creation of a carefully curated hadith number → matn → source reference table.
Such a curated table would immediately improve every downstream analysis, including lexical statistics, repeated phrase discovery, source abbreviation analysis, narrator extraction, stylometric studies, transmission-network analysis, and future computational linguistics research.
For this reason, scholarly corpus curation is expected to provide substantially greater long-term benefit than continued refinement of individual parsing heuristics.

title: Computational Corpus
text: Despite the remaining areas requiring curation, the current Version 1.0 corpus already supports meaningful quantitative investigation. The following chapters present three examples of corpus-wide analyses generated directly from the current DuckDB corpus: descriptive statistics of the matn, empirical discovery of repeated phrases, and analysis of source abbreviations and narrator information.

title: Repeated Matn Phrases
text:
Script 04b extends the lexical analyses of Script 04 by identifying repeated multi-word expressions occurring throughout the matn. Rather than relying on predefined phrase lists or manually curated formulae, the procedure discovers repeated expressions directly from the corpus using an unsupervised n-gram approach. This allows recurring language to emerge naturally from the text itself and provides a quantitative basis for investigating formulaic expression in Kanz al-Ummāl.
Unlike Script 04, which counts a predefined set of devotional formulae, Script 04b discovers repeated language empirically from the corpus itself. Together, the two scripts provide complementary approaches: one hypothesis-driven and one data-driven.



The script generates the following analytical products. Separate phrase-length files are intentionally produced because they are easier to inspect manually than a single large review table.



Script 04b analytical outputs






Output
Description




kanz_04b_repeated_matn_phrases_all
Master table of all retained repeated phrases from 2-word through 8-word expressions.


kanz_04b_repeated_2grams
Review table containing repeated two-word phrases.


kanz_04b_repeated_3grams
Review table containing repeated three-word phrases.


kanz_04b_repeated_4grams
Review table containing repeated four-word phrases.


kanz_04b_repeated_5grams
Review table containing repeated five-word phrases.


kanz_04b_repeated_6grams
Review table containing repeated six-word phrases.

Review table containing repeated seven-word phrases.

Review table containing repeated eight-word phrases.

Hadith-level occurrences of every retained repeated phrase, including count within the hadith and matn context.


kanz_04b_run_summary
Overall processing summary, parameters, retained phrase counts, and output file locations.





The master repeated-phrase table, hadith-level phrase-hit table, and run summary are also mirrored into DuckDB as:

kanz_04b_repeated_matn_phrases_all
kanz_04b_hadith_repeated_phrase_hits
kanz_04b_run_summary

NoteKey finding



Script 04b demonstrates that the current corpus can support data-driven discovery of recurring language. The analysis does not begin with a predefined list of formulae; instead, it identifies repeated phrases directly from the matn and then organizes them into reviewable files by phrase length.

title: Repeated Matn Phrases
text:
3 Preliminary Results
Script 04b examined 43,846 matn-bearing reports and generated contiguous word sequences from 2-word through 8-word phrases. To reduce noise, a phrase was retained only if it occurred at least 2 times in the corpus and appeared in at least 2 separate hadith reports.
The analysis produced 6,009,654 unique candidate phrases before filtering and retained 567,715 repeated phrases for review. These retained phrases generated 2,071,563 hadith-level phrase-hit rows, allowing each recurring expression to be traced back to the reports in which it appears.

The retained-phrase counts decline as phrase length increases. This is expected: short two-word and three-word sequences are common because they often reflect ordinary grammatical or lexical combinations, whereas longer repeated phrases are more selective and more likely to represent recognizable formulae, stock expressions, or recurring narrative patterns.

title: Repeated Matn Phrases
text:
8 Interpretation
Unlike the manually curated devotional expressions counted in Script 04, this procedure makes no assumptions about which phrases are linguistically or religiously significant. Repeated language is discovered directly from the corpus itself. This distinction matters because recurrent expressions in hadith literature may include standard openings, narrative transitions, oaths, quotation formulae, supplications, and other conventional wording that would be difficult to enumerate exhaustively beforehand.
The separate review files organized by phrase length also support practical human inspection. Short phrases are useful for identifying common lexical and grammatical pairings, while longer repeated phrases are more likely to reveal complete formulaic expressions or stereotyped narrative patterns. The 2-gram and 5-gram examples therefore serve different purposes: the former gives a broad view of local word association, while the latter begins to expose more meaningful repeated language.
At the same time, these results should be interpreted as phrase candidates rather than final linguistic categories. Repetition alone does not prove that a phrase has a specific rhetorical, theological, or transmission function. The value of Script 04b is that it narrows the search space and produces an empirical phrase inventory that can be reviewed, classified, and reused in later analyses.






ImportantPractical implication



Script 04b turns the corpus into a source of candidate formulae. Rather than asking a reviewer to invent a phrase list from memory, the pipeline can surface repeated expressions automatically and then allow human reviewers to decide which ones are linguistically or religiously meaningful.

title: Repeated Matn Phrases
text:
9 Building on These Results
The repeated-phrase inventory provides a foundation for several accessible computational linguistic extensions:

Formulaic language discovery: identifying recurring expressions that appear across multiple hadith reports.
Phrase dictionaries: building reviewable lists of repeated expressions for later annotation and classification.
Collocation analysis: studying which words tend to occur together more often than expected.
Narrative-pattern analysis: identifying repeated openings, transitions, quotations, and closing formulae.
Stylometric comparison: measuring how formula density differs across subsets of the corpus.
Search and retrieval: enabling phrase-based search rather than only single-word search.
Corpus comparison: comparing repeated expressions in Kanz al-Ummāl with other hadith collections or Classical Arabic corpora.
Machine-learning preparation: producing phrase-level features that can be used in clustering, topic discovery, or semantic modelling.

title: Repeated Matn Phrases
text:
10 Summary Assessment
Script 04b is a proof-of-concept for data-driven formulaic language discovery in the preliminary Kanz al-Ummāl corpus. It does not solve the linguistic classification problem by itself, but it creates the empirical foundation needed for later classification. The result is a practical bridge between raw text extraction and higher-level computational linguistics: repeated language can now be found, counted, reviewed, and traced back to individual hadith reports.

title: Towards the Computational Study of the Prophetic Traditions
text:
The analyses presented in this report demonstrate that a computational corpus can be constructed from Kanz al-Ummāl and used for reproducible quantitative analysis. The greatest remaining challenge is no longer computational infrastructure, but scholarly refinement of the underlying hadith number → matn → source reference table.




A curated corpus would allow reliable identification of common vocabulary, rare vocabulary, lexical diversity, and recurring expressions across the entire collection.



Repeated phrase analysis can identify formulaic language and recurring patterns directly from the Arabic text.



Al-Muttaqī al-Hindī arranged Kanz al-Ummāl according to subject matter. A curated corpus would permit comparison of vocabulary, phraseology, grammatical features, and style across belief, worship, ethics, commerce, family life, governance, and other major subjects.



Studying recurring combinations of words can reveal characteristic expressions and conceptual associations.



Computational methods can identify reports that naturally cluster according to vocabulary, phrase usage, or linguistic style.



A computational corpus can support quantitative studies of particles, roots, verb forms, verbal nouns (maṣādir), prefixes, suffixes, and other grammatical features.






Kanz al-Ummāl is based upon the earlier work of al-Suyūṭī, while al-Muttaqī al-Hindī reorganized the material by subject. Quantitative analysis of abbreviated source citations may help reconstruct which earlier collections appear to have contributed most heavily.



Linking the curated corpus to the original collections would permit large-scale studies of narrator activity, transmission pathways, and relationships among narrators extending beyond Kanz al-Ummāl itself.






Statistical summaries, corpus linguistics, computational linguistics, and information retrieval can reveal vocabulary, recurring expressions, thematic organization, and linguistic variation across more than forty-six thousand reports.



A reliable computational corpus provides a foundation for semantic analysis, narrator and source network analysis, statistical modeling, and machine-assisted discovery of textual patterns.






ImportantA Long-Term Research Resource



The objective of this project is not simply to create another digital edition of Kanz al-Ummāl. Rather, it is to establish a carefully curated computational corpus that serves as a durable research resource.
Every improvement to the underlying hadith number → matn → source reference table immediately strengthens every downstream analysis, from descriptive corpus statistics to studies of language, transmission, compilation history, and the broader computational study of the Prophetic traditions.
